comments.title: Emotions Again
comments.content:
This is a leftover from last week.

You said that there are internal and external emotions. How do we tell which is which?

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content: That takes a lot of practice. Meditation is a good way to get there -- with regular meditation, you get enough self-knowledge that you can tell the "feel" or "flavor" of your own emotional patterns, and an emotion from outside will have a different "feel" or "flavor" to it -- and the regular practice of banishing rituals will make you resistant to outside emotions; other than that, I don't know of any short cuts.

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content:
That's more than enough; at least there is a way. Thank you very much.

I want to say that if there are outlandish ideas you have and you don't want to taint your reputation, you can write under a pseudonym or make arrangements for posthumous publication.

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content: Oh, I gave up on being respectable a long time ago, when I decided to pursue a career as a public occultist rather than trying for an academic position. I've never regretted it -- and one reason for that is that it gives me the freedom to say absolutely anything, no matter how outlandish.

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content: (Offlist) I meant that comment as a joke, actually. I'll discuss such things as time and my interests permit.

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content:
Hi Packshaud,
I find just asking myself if a sudden or strong emotion is even mine is often enough to shut it down.
Maxine Rogers

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content: I often do this as well. It seems to be more useful when I notice the emotion coming before memories--I think what I remember after an emotion coming on its own could be by association. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content:
To be TMI Girl, growing up with ye olde hormonal cycle has really gotten me into the habit of doing that when I think it might be a factor--"does this really bug me, or am I PMSing?"--and life with anxiety also helps in a similar way: "Is this an actual thing I'm worried about? Or is my brain just being chemically ridiculous?"

As you say, questioning itself really seems to help a lot of the time.

replies.title: Re: Emotions Again
replies.content: YES! I struggle with anger. It's helpful to think of the anger as its own thing-- a lurking creature. The anger is just "out there". It settles on me, and then finds reasons-to-be-angry.  Turning on it and picking it apart in that light often takes care of the problem. Also helps me treat members of my family better. I'm not angry *at them*. They just happen to be the nearest targets, most of the time.

comments.title: Mirror Cleaning
comments.content: Do you have any opinions on how to clean mirrors? I ask because I use vinegar and you have made comments about vinegar's effects on the non-corporeal. I also ask because of the concepts of vampires and mirror-divination.

replies.title: Re: Mirror Cleaning
replies.content: Vinegar's great for mirror cleaning, metaphysically as well as physically -- and that's true if you're using the mirror for magical purposes, too. You want it to be clear of lingering energies from previous uses. If you're going to use the mirror for magic, rinse it with water once you've cleaned it with vinegar, and you'll be fine.

replies.title: Re: Mirror Cleaning
replies.content: As we're on the subject, what are traditional magical properties or uses for mirrors (at least according to the traditions you know well)? Mirrors have such a heavy presence in Shinto, and there are a variety of common symbolic explanations as to their meaning there; but I'm curious as to whether there's perhaps something more practical going on as well.

replies.title: Re: Mirror Cleaning
replies.content: In western magic, they're mostly used as a tool for clairvoyance. I'm not familiar with the full range of their uses in Shinto; perhaps one of our practitioners of that faith will comment.

replies.title: Re: Mirror Cleaning
replies.content: I don’t know how magical they might be, but if you have hard water, the foam version of Scrubbing Bubbles bathroom cleaner cleans mirrors better than Windex OR vinegar.  It seems to repel toothpaste spots for a day or two and makes the mirror seem brighter, somehow.  (Maybe it IS magic!). I found this out when we were out of vinegar and my son had taken the Windex off somewhere, probably for use in eldritch rites to summon the Great Clean Ones, and now I save the Scrubbing Bubbles foam for the mirror.

replies.title: Re: Mirror Cleaning
replies.content: Very many keks at the "Great Clean Ones". Well done.

comments.title: Marian apparitions
comments.content:
JMG, Happy Holidays to you -

I'm wondering what you think of Marian apparitions, which have continued to occur through the 19th and 20th centuries, right up until this past June in Knock, Ireland - a 12 old boy visiting Lourdes, I believe, evidently received divine word that there'd be a Marian manifestation at Knock, and when the prophesied date came, sure enough, there appeared - witnessed by many - the "dancing, elongated sun" associated with many modern supernatural Marian events. Though the Church hasn't signed off on all the Marian apparitions, many of the Church-sanctioned ones, modern-day ones included, have evidently involved miraculous healings. I think in some ways, the Marian phenomena is an echo of a Medieval spirituality in which divine miracles occur with some regularity. Talk about flying in the face of modern cultural conventions ...

Harking back to your speculation that the resonating power of Jesus Christ may not be what it once was - a speculation which made sense to me, given everything that's happened to Christianity post- Nietzsche - I'm thinking of some aspiring Christians I've known who've told me that they seem to they seem to receive zip when praying/meditating on Jesus .... but they emphatically receive when praying to and meditating on the figure and image of Mary. So I'm wondering if perhaps Mary is the evolving "new face" of Christianity. This would compliment the relatively recent awareness of the "Eternal Feminine", perhaps a spiritual high point of such in contrast to the risibility of much of contemporary feminism. This might also prove to be a more feminine aspect of Christianity that could eventually result in an ecosophia-friendly outlook to nature, the earth, and our responsibilities therein. Perhaps the veneration of Mary might evolve into something that goes considerably beyond the scope of what has been thought of as Christianity?

Question 2 - Is there such a thing as a "Druid mystic"? Or are all Druids expected to be aspiring mages?

Thanks, Will M

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
1) Mary really does seem to have serious power behind her, and manifests fairly often. I'm not sure if that's because she's in the process of becoming the core figure of a new Christianity, or whether it's simply because so many Catholic mystics focus on her so intently that they've built up a very large pool of force. We'll just have to see.

2) Magic is actually a fairly modest part of the Druid Revival tradition -- I've found no trace of magical practices in modern Druidry before the collapse of the Golden Dawn, which sent a lot of refugees into Druid orders. There have been, and still probably are, far more mystics among Druids than mages, and of course a vast number of people who don't really take either of the intensive paths, but simply find Druid nature worship a valid way to relate to the spiritual side of being.

replies.title: Re: mystic druidry
replies.content: Has much been written about mystic Druidry? Any authors to keep an eye out for? I know about Ross Nichols, but not a lot about the older stuff.

replies.title: Re: mystic druidry
replies.content: There isn't a lot. Most of the earlier Druid writings focus on philosophy or on symbolism -- the practical dimensions seem to have been passed on by word of mouth, and under the oaths of secrecy usual at that time. You might try Lewis Spence's The Mysteries of Britain, and of course there's plenty of raw material in Barddas.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
Interesting conversation, folks! Getting back to the issue of mass hysteria vs. real mass visions, if I recall correctly, in one of his books on UFOs, Jacques Vallee provided a detailed description of the Marian phenomenon at Lourdes and in particular a miraculous healing described by an initially sceptical medical doctor in the late 19th century (in brief, the instant healing a Lourdes was medically impossible and the autopsy of the healed person, once he eventually died, revealed extraordinary knitting of a severely shattered femur); also, if I recall correctly, John A. Keel had drawn parallels between the visions reported at Fatima and the behaviour of UFOs.
I guess an important question is: how can we differentiate between authentic visions and mass hysteria?
For what it’s worth, I have no problem believing in the spiritual reality of Marian visions and in the faery-nature of many UFO sightings. But how strong fears and/or religious faith of groups of people can play into this is so far a mystery to me. Hopefully the books suggested last week will help me!

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
I forgot to mention that the last apparition was announced in advance. A considerable portion of the photographic cameras existing in the date were possibly there; there are photos of the crowds, but none of the phenomenon. If it was phosphene, the photos would exist.

You could have done worse, of course; at least your explanation was not Joe "Owls" Nickell.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
Your observations parallel my experience. I'm Orthodox, not Catholic, but we also are very fond of the Mother of God. My one paltry unambiguous mystical experience was of the Theotokos. She answered a thorny but perhaps somewhat frivolous question I'd had. I still don't understand the answer, but the right course of action was clear. If I had to put it in modern terms, I would emphatically say that it had something to do with the divine feminine.

When Orthodox get together and compare notes on direct mystical experience (and part of the reason I am Orthodox today, is that this seems so *normal* in the church), you rarely get "Jesus appeared to me"(and when you do, they are often terrifying). But you get lots of angels, saints, and the Theotokos (Mary). I figured it was simply because we find Christ intimidating. But the saints were all regular, fallible people once, and are sympathetic, relatable.

I do not know if it is theologically sound, but the way I picture it is... when we pray, when we reach out to God, we are sort of opening up a channel for God's grace to flow into the world. That is not a small commitment-- sort of like volunteering to be a lightning rod. It's like when Christ spoke to the rich young man: sell all you have, leave everything you know, and follow Me, right? You don't get to keep anything for yourself. Most of us quail before that reality. The saints... it's like they are reaching out to meet us halfway (or more than!). Most of us need some considerable hand-holding when it comes to channeling Divine Grace. They apparently like to help us out with that.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
methylethyl
>> ... you rarely get "Jesus appeared to me"(and when you do, they are often terrifying). But you get lots of angels, saints, and the Theotokos (Mary). I figured it was simply because we find Christ intimidating. But the saints were all regular, fallible people once, and are sympathetic, relatable.<<
I'm not sure if the metaphysical appearance of Mary (the Theotokos) has any solid reason to be less terrifying than a Jesus appearance. First, Jesus was 100 % human, albeit a human operating at peak spiritual efficiency, so to speak - a manifestation/exemplar to which all us humans can attain. Mary, I imagine, serves the same role. Remember, in certain esoteric circles, Mary is believed to also have been immaculately conceived as was Jesus. To the extent that Jesus is a terrifying, otherworldly metaphysical force, I think the same might be said about Mary. Yet it's Mary who continues to manifest with considerable power, not so much Jesus. Why?

I can speculate (based on JMG's speculation that Jesus might be a "receding god") that Mary might be serving as "the other face of Jesus", to be sure, the pronounced feminine aspect that's slowly but surely coming to the fore as we enter a new age of metaphysical reality. In other words, Mary serves as the twin soul of Jesus, the feminine aspect. Maybe this is the reason why Mary seems much more present than does Jesus in our contemporary world - but I can only speculate.

Thanks, Will M

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
I am no longer a Christian, so take this with a grain of salt.

Jesus is a half-god, and Mary is--or at least was--fully human. For this reason She (why wouldn't I use capitals for Her as well) might be more relatable.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
Re--"in certain esoteric circles..."

I would note that the Immaculate Conception--that Mary was conceived without sin--is official doctrine for Catholics. I suppose Catholics are only one esoteric circle, but I usually think of that as referring to smaller groups.

This doctrine has one extremely helpful effect in my opinion: it disconnects original sin and sex. With Jesus being both free of original sin and born of a virgin, the implication that sin is basically sex-related is an plausible interpretation. With Catholic doctrine, that isn't a plausible interpretation--and since think it's both wrong and unhelpful, I'm glad that Catholic doctrine forecloses that interpretation.

SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content: Well, I can't say if misuse of the life-force via immoderate sexual activity is *the* major cause of sin - "sin" being anything that blocks the divine from entering human consciousness - but I'd wager it's a major karma-generator.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
That belief strikes me as very, very strange. The genitals are about 3% of the body, and sex is about 3% of life -- the way that the Christian tradition obsesses about that little portion, when Jesus himself had some very different ideas about what sins mattered most (consult the Gospels for details), has always made me scratch my head.

In karmic terms, according to the traditions I've studied, sex is no more significant than eating, or defecating, or any other normal biological activity. Of course you can involve it in nasty karmic situations, but -- for example -- what's wrong with adultery isn't that it involves sex, it's that you're breaking a solemn promise by doing so. The sex itself is morally neutral.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content: JMG, overall I agree with you, but I did say "misuse" of the life force as in over-indulgence, obsession, a tactic to shut out reality - just the same with over-indulgence with eating.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content: Most people spend much more time eating than having sex, and I suspect the amount of overindulgence and obsession that goes into food vastly outweighs the amount that goes into sex -- and yet it's sex that gets all the rhetoric. I think there's something really odd going on there.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
I assume sex gets more airtime than food in most cultures, because the taboos surrounding it are a non-trivial part of the glue that holds society together. Yes, there are lots of different sets of rules about it, but every culture has rules-- and those rules tend to regulate who is responsible for the resulting children.

Overeating is damaging to your soul in much the same way that the wrong use of sex is... but in a situation where food is plentiful, it only harms the person doing the indulging. No children result. Thus: fewer taboos. I would expect the sin of gluttony to be regarded as much, much more serious in any situation where food is NOT plentiful, and overindulgence results in others going hungry.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content: By that logic, wouldn't gay and lesbian sex, or straight sex where one or both parties are infertile, be no more of an issue that overeating?

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
Yes. I oversimplified.

The care of children *is* a major concern of most, if not all, cultures, and is possibly the main reason for having a lot of rules about sex. But sex does seem to affect cultural cohesion and social structures as well, even where children don't, or can't, result. Institutionalized male homosexuality in ancient Greece (and to a lesser extent, in the Roman empire) certainly wasn't resulting in children, but had a non-trivial effect on male power structures. Probably also on the status of women (in a negative way).

So there are also non-reproduction-related reasons for having rules to maintain the sexual status quo in any given culture, if you have an interest in preserving that culture. They may not be as obvious, or as important, as those regulating reproduction. I don't know.

As an aside, I don't think the Bible has anything whatever to say about lesbian sex. I've always wondered about that: was it simply less disruptive to Judaic culture than male/male sex?

I think you are right that in current American culture, there is an odd imbalance where sex is concerned. I suspect it has to do with the strong Gnostic streak in modern Protestantism, but it's hard to say. I know what my own church teaches, but we're such a small minority that it has no bearing on what the wider culture thinks.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:

That might be testable - I suspect that said goddess *would* answer to the name Madoka Kaname, so somebody who's got a stronger sense of Mary Theotokos than I do could track down some of the Madoka Magica fanart (or just watch the original show, if you're willing to spend six to seven hours watching moving pictures) and see if any of it feels Marian. (Fair warning if you decide to do this: this involves brushing fandom in general and Japanese fandom in particular. Keep your safe search firmly on.)

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
Alas, I can only speak to it from an Orthodox perspective, where we believe that Jesus is fully God and fully Man, both. Unlike Catholics, the immaculate conception of Mary isn't dogma for us, though I think most Orthodox believe it anyway.

Mary-- for us, not the same role. More like... she's the model of what all Christians should be striving for: ultimate self-abnegation. "May it be done to me according to Your will." Which *is* fairly terrifying if you take the time to think about it. But still, she's not ALSO the eternal creator of the cosmos.

I don't see it as Jesus receding and Mary stepping to the fore. I think we're just very fond of her. And I wouldn't discount JMG's explanation, either. If ritual devotion can store up a well of power that lets her manifest (I wouldn't know), then boy, does she have a deep well to draw from! I don't think it is coincidental that she spoke to me at a Paraklesis service (where we get together and sing a long, long hymn to the Virgin, during the Dormition fast, which commemorates her death).

I think in terms of apparitions and things, she's *always* seemed more present, and it may be a function of seeming more accessible to us. This was so pronounced in the middle ages, that the Reformation practically threw her out of the church, because they felt the extreme devotion to Mary was a form of idolatry. You'll hardly hear her mentioned at all in Protestant churches, except as a character in the Nativity story.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content: Can you elaborate on the terrifying part?  More accurately, what kind of terror?  I've been keeping an outsider's eye on the evolution of Christianity, and "Christ is now terrifying to Christians" is consistent with a couple of possibilities I see - but those possibilities suggest different reasons for it, and that might affect how it comes across.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
I'm going to say this badly, but... terrifying not in the sense of man-eating lions, or bogey men. Terrifying in a mere-mortals-encountering-the-infinite, or God-is-a-cleansing-fire kind of way. Where one is made keenly, inescapably, aware of Christ's boundless love, and also of how radically unworthy one is of it?

I'm afraid that's as close as I can get to it. And it's not quite right.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content: As a lapsed Catholic myself, I find this whole conversation fascinating. Just about the only Christian practices I’ve retained are prayers to a few favorite saints: mainly Mary, Joseph (for career-related things), and the archangel Michael (for protection for my children). I’ve often wondered about the theology/metaphysics behind it, but at the end of the day it works and They don’t seem to mind that I haven’t been to mass in years and have slowly been getting involved with druidry for a while now...

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content: There are a lot of Christian Druids, and there are also a lot of traditional Druid rituals that borrow from the Christian pantheon -- Ross Nichols' original seasonal rituals for the Order of Bards Ovates and Druids, for example, invoke archangels and saints as well as Celtic divinities. (That kind of syncretism is very common in indigenous faiths -- and Druidry is, after all, an indigenous nature religion of the English-speaking countries.) So you're well within the range of familiar druidical variation.

replies.title: Re: Marian apparitions
replies.content:
Even as a still-practicing Christian, I find this fascinating! It's a source of deep personal amusement that when various ecumenical meetings get together, trying to suss out what are the deep, common, spiritual truths that all religions can agree on, they always jump for these big, abstract spiritual concepts that don't make sense to anyone but theologians.

But if you take even a quick survey of major (and minor) religions nearly everywhere, the things they mostly have in common are a belief in bodiless powers and beings, both major and minor, good ones and bad ones, that sometimes interfere in the lives of us mere mortals. Angels, demons, spirits, ancestors, etc. That, apparently, is part of the universal human experience. It doesn't make sense to write that off as superstition.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Being able to see and hear one's recently deceased spouse, if the marriage has been of any length and depth, is extremely common. Tokienguy's grandfather is neither delusional nor abnormal.

From anecdotes and conversations alone, I would guess that more than half, maybe even three-fourths or more of elderly recent widows and widowers have had that experience, and it often continues throughout the remainder of the survivor's life.

Most folk will never ever volunteer information about it outside their immediate family, and often not even within it, for fear of being thought crazy and losing what little credibility they still have with their offspring. But if one is sincerely curious, genuinely sympathetic, and clearly does not think such experiences need to be suppressed or refuted at any cost, often the stories will begin to flow as trust builds.

Ordinary mirrors, on a dressing table or a wall, seem to act as a sort of easy site for this; but also the deceased is often seen sitting in his/her favorite chair, or place at the dinner table, or -- and this really spooks some widow(er)s! -- even lying in bed next to the surviving spouse.

I don't know of anything published on this, but I've been talking to people about it, on and off, for decades. It's a real experience.

I suspect that funeral rituals were first invented to keep this sort of appearance from happening. I also suspect they're largely ineffectual, at least in the modern Western world.

There's a lot more than can be said here, especially about customary practices and rituals. Some of these practices and rituals are meant to keep the channels of communication open, not shut them down.

comments.title: Synchronicity
comments.content:
I'm agnostic on the question of magic. On one hand, I'm not hubristic enough to say I understand consciousness, but on the other, working magic is a pretty big claim. However, it's obvious that a properly focused mind is a powerful thing, and whether magic works or not, what you call Raspberry Jam Principle certainly does.

It seems to me that working with associations in highly concentrated state might, due to RJP, sensitize your mind to those in everyday life. Coincidences that would normally go unnoticed or merely elicit a "huh, that was weird" shrug, would be more likely to get noticed and labeled meaningful. So, the actual question: Is this in your experience a potential pitfall, and how do you distinguish between actual synchronicities and false alarms?

I'll probably start experimenting with blessings once I've trained my concentration some more. In a way it doesn't matter if those actually do anything. Still, I'd rather not get caught up in cosmic dramas of my own creation.

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content:
If something happens twice, it is a coincidence; if three or more times, you should start paying attention.

You can borrow Synchronicity: an acausal connecting principle by Carl Jung from the Open Library.

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content:
Hmm. When you say "working magic is a pretty big claim," what do you mean by "working magic"? It's been my experience that a great many people who are agnostic or skeptical about magic don't actually know what it is, and are relying on pop-culture notions that have nothing to do with the realities of the subject. It's rather as though somebody insisted "working science is a pretty big claim," and it turned out that by "working science" they meant the things that mad scientists do in 1950s horror movies...

Magic is the art and science of causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will. (That's Dion Fortune's definition, btw.) When you say that working magic is a pretty big claim, are you suggesting that it isn't possible to cause changes in consciousness in accordance with will? Or that emotionally charged symbols and ritual psychodrama can't cause changes in consciousness? Or what?

As for getting caught up in cosmic dramas of your own creation, yes, that's a risk, and most people who teach occultism have to deal with students getting into that. It's also a risk to deliberately blind yourself to actual meaning in the events around you. The opposite of one bad idea, as usual, is another bad idea; the middle ground between them offers many more possibilities. See you there! ;-)

replies.title: Re: Synchronicity
replies.content:
I have modest experience in meditation, enough to have verified that carefully forming intentions while highly concentrated has power over consciousness. Meditation seems to gather much of the normally quite disjointed mind together, and in such state, it's possible for its parts to reach an unusually broad consensus of what they want to do. Symbolism and ritual psychodrama do empower both the unification of mind and forming of strong intentions, I'm sure.

I suspect that this process could even create magical-seeming results. A proper ritual to hex some social group might supercharge your ability to read its weak points. Opportunities of attack would seem so obvious you just exploited them without even thinking about it, cutting words were easy to choose, and your nonverbal communication would help out without conscious prompting. It might look like you just did a working and things pretty much happened on their own.

Working magic wouldn't be Harry Potter stuff. It's causing changes in world in accordance to your will, without any plausible causal link. Bringing luck to others, changing the minds of people half a planet away, binding Kavanaugh and so on. One doesn't violate scientific worldview, though it's rarely something people think about if they're really into scientific materialism. Other would be something profoundly weird.

Ultimately I need to see if I can push things to the point where the model described above just doesn't work anymore, while keeping a clear head in the process. Don't know what will happen, but I'm sure it will be interesting!

comments.title: Mirror Magic
comments.content:
Hello!

Today you mentioned mirror magic.

(1) What is it all about?

(2) What are the occult dimensions of mirrors?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Mirror Magic
replies.content:
1) Mirrors are commonly used in some branches of Western occultism as a tool for visionary experience -- the same sort of thing other traditions do with crystal balls, or bowls of water, or various other things. Many people find that by looking into a reflective surface and focusing through it, as though it was a window, that with practice they can enter a mild trance state in which images appear in the surface.

2) Oh, about six inches wide by nine inches tall. ;-)

Seriously? Magically speaking, a mirror is just a piece of glass or clear plastic with a reflective layer on one surface. They don't have any particular occult dimension beyond that, unless you deliberately consecrate and use one as a working tool.

replies.title: Re: Mirror Magic
replies.content:
Ada Goodrich-Freer (under her pseudonym "Miss X") wrote a number of very intriguing accounts of how seeing things in a mirror or crystal ball came to her naturally as a child, and how she learned to use that ability very productively. See her volume "Essays in Psychical Research" (1899), which can be downloaded here (and elsewhere):

archive.org/details/b24884121/page/n3

The chapters "On the Faculty of Crystal-Gazing" and "How It Came Into My Head" are the most relevant to this subject, but the whole book repays close reading. (She was something of a journalist and a story-teller, and she had to survive by her wits most of her life, but she had a first-rate mind for understanding a wide range of outside-the-box experiences and phenomena.)

Other worth-while authors are Theodore Besterman ("Crystal Gazing") and the fairy-tale author and folklorist, Andrew Lang (many essays in many places).

replies.content: There were actually quite a few variants of the Tree back in the day, in terms of Path arrangement and even the placement of the Spheres, and in the usual way of things those gradually settled down to one option that works. comments.title: Magic and Children

replies.title: Re: Magic and Children
replies.content:
Sigh. No matter how many times I repeat myself on this subject, people take it to extremes. No, it is not true that magic and kids don't mix. There are specific kinds of magic that it's not safe to do around kids, because they can be influenced in harmful ways. You can meditate, practice divination, do any kind of natural magic, and do basic magical practices such as the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram and the Middle Pillar exercise when you have children -- don't do the latter two in a space where they spend a lot of time until they're past 3 or so, but that's about it.

The problems come in when you do serious ceremonial magic -- invoking spirits, consecrating talismans or working tools, doing anything that involves calling down divine energies at high levels of intensity. You should not do those at all until your children are older than 3 or so, and you shouldn't do them with kids in the house until they're 7 or so. (When they're between 3 and 7, do serious ritual when the kids are at Grandma's or preschool or what have you.)

Is that a little clearer?

replies.title: Re: Spiritual practice in preparation for travel
replies.content: Journaling might be a very good thing to do, since that's an effective way to get down past the surface layer of your mind. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: what do you make of the popularity of the meme; "the medium is the message"?  I see a lot of intellectual types who skirt the edges of occultism say this maxim quite a bit, enough so that it appears to be something of a thing.  Any insights you have on this are much appreciated!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good heavens, is that old chestnut being dragged back out again? That was one of the favorite tag lines of Marshall McLuhan, who was a fashionable bad-boy intellectual in the 1960s; his point was that the medium you choose to communicate a message is not value- or content-free, but communicates things by the way it structures the communication process. (For example, television requires passivity on the part of the viewer -- all you can do is sit there and watch -- and so the act of watching television communicates the message that you, the watcher, are a passive witness, and it's other people somewhere else who actually do things.) It's a valid point but can be overused, and it's also been old hat for fifty years...

replies.title: Re: Manly Hall and "breadcrumbs"
replies.content:
Thank you for this! No, I didn't know that these were available.

As for your broader question, that's fair. I've discussed in general terms what magic can do in terms of personal development, and stressed that this takes a different form for each individual -- that's why the highest rank of attainment in the Golden Dawn system has the title Ipsissimus, which means "most completely oneself." The point of magic is not to turn people into identical cookie-cutter mages who all achieve the same things, it's to make it possible for each person to achieve whatever unique potential for magnificence they themselves contain. Thus the changes my practice has brought about in my life -- which are considerable -- are not the changes your practice will bring about in yours.

Does a seed know what the tree that will grow from it will look like? That's the challenge faced by the would-be mage who thinks, as you do, that you can have the end in mind at the beginning of the path. That's one of the reasons why I don't ever try to talk anyone into taking up magic, and don't offer reasons when people want to know why they should. If you have to convince yourself to take up magic, you probably should do something else instead, because those reasons won't keep you going through the hard dry times. What gets you through them is the same gut-level, even cell-level drive that pushes open the seed in the ground and drives roots down and shoots up.

As for deeper teachings, I'm looking into that possibility now. I've already published some of those, though in a somewhat unexpected form.

replies.title: Re: Procrastination
replies.content:
Hubby and I were discussing this earlier today, and we've come to the conclusion that motivation during the dark time of the year (northern hemisphere) is more difficult than in the spring or fall when the daylight is more balanced with night. Summer solstice usually feels like we never get enough sleep.

replies.title: Re: Procrastination
replies.content: You need to figure out what's behind the procrastination. The habit of procrastinating, like so many other habits, usually has roots in childhood experiences and in emotional needs, and until you figure those out, just trying to suppress it by by brute force isn't going to help much. One way that works well for me is journaling. In a notebook set aside for the purpose, have a series of conversations with your procrastination; ask it questions and write down whatever answers come to mind, without censoring; let it ask you questions, too, and answer them as honestly as you can. It may take a while to get down to the roots of your procrastination, but in my experience, that's a far more effective way to go about it.

replies.title: Re: Arthur E Powell
replies.content:
Powell was a man of his time, with the prejudices of his nationality and class. He was also trying to defend occultism against accusations of sexual immorality, thus the moralistic preaching.

Yes, you can certainly replace the incense with a fan, or some other emblem of air. Some of the orders related to AODA that use the same basic ritual format do this sort of thing routinely.

replies.title: Re: First draft of Mythos SOP
replies.content:
I trust you meant the Gaelic pantheon, rather than the Garlic pantheon -- though I suppose the latter would be powerful against vampires!

As to the tentacular SoP, I'd use a different set of words for the Elemental Cross, and a different set of gestures. One of Lovecraft's stories has a great general invocation:

"From the wells of night to the gulfs of space, and from the gulfs of space to the wells of night, ever the praises of Great Cthulhu, of Tsathoggua, and of Him Who is not to be Named. Ever Their praises, and abundance to the Black Goat of the Woods. Iä! Shub-Ne'hurrath! The Goat with a Thousand Young!"

Combine this with the following gestures:

"From the wells of night to the gulfs of space" -- sweep your arms up from your sides to above your head, joining them palm to palm.

"and from the gulfs of space to the wells of night" -- draw the joined hands down to heart level.

"ever the praises of Great Cthulhu, of Tsathoggua, and of Him Who is not to be Named" -- extend your hands straight out in the form of a cross, palm up.

"Ever Their praises, and abundance to the Black Goat of the Woods" -- cross your arms over your chest, left arm over right, each hand in front of the opposite shoulder.

"Iä! Shub-Ne'hurrath! The Goat with a Thousand Young!" -- uncross them and let them fall to your sides, angled slightly outward, palms forward.

The elemental invocations are mostly suitable, though it's not done to speak the name of the King -- it would be "In the great and unspeakable Name." For the others, I'd simply say "In the great name (name), I invoke the element (element), its creatures, its spirits, and its powers," and so on.

There is no deity for Spirit Within -- that's the seed of light within you, not an outside deity -- so no, you don't invoke Azathoth there, or anywhere else for that matter! You could vibrate Awen, since there are Druids who worship the Black Goat in the mythos -- Robert Bloch's "Notebook Found in a Deserted House" has them, for example -- or you could go full tentacle and vibrate "Cthulhu fhtagn!"

(In my fictive world the Plateau of Leng is east of the Himalayan plateau and forms the border region between China, Laos, and Myanmar; the ruins of Sarkomand are thus in far northeastern Laos.)

replies.title: Re: First draft of Mythos SOP
replies.content:
A very good point! I'm also fond of Coast Salish languages such as Lushootseed, which strictly speaking have no nouns at all -- instead, you tack s'- on front of a verbal phrase and it functions like a noun.

The habit of using single words with lots of affixes to replace what would be a sentence in English seems to be fairly widespread among Native American languages. I recall a conversation I had some years back when I was learning Takelma, a southwestern Oregon native language, and an online friend of mine was learning Ioway, and we noted jointly that in both languages "I go wherever there is dancing" was a single word...

replies.title: Re: First draft of Mythos SOP
replies.content:
I expect that this depends very much on the individual Narragansett.

I know of one elderly Narragansett (now deceased for at least thirty years) who would not pass on any of the herbal lore which she had been taught as a child to anyone who was not of pure Narragansett ancestry--not even to her own daughter-in-law (who was mixed Black and Irish). She would not so much as say "Hello" to any White person if she could help it. She kept vividly alive in her memory, so I was told, all the many wrongs the White settlers had inflicted on the Narragansetts from the early 1600s down to her own days.

On the other hand, there are Narragansett politicians who will cut whatever bargins they can with Whites to advance the interests of the tribe (and their own interests, too, of course, in good Rhode Island fashion). I dobut that they would mind, and might even encourage studying Narragansett with an eye to the tribe's future advantage.

I have heard that there are no fluent native speakers of Narragansett left, and there have not been any for many generations. They, like outsiders, have to rely on early records of the language in their efforts to recreate fluency in it. So far as I know, everyone begins with Roger Williams' Narragansett grammar and vocabulary, "A Key to the Language of America" (1643), available here in digital form:

archive.org/details/keyintolanguageo00will_0/page/n1

replies.title: Re: When/should Regardie's "The Golden Dawn" be followed

comments.content:
Forgive me for the following not-at-all-related questions: whenever I come with a question, I’m inspired by others as I’m reading with a further question!

So, first the question I came with: having watched the growing environmental chaos we’ve brought upon ourselves up close and personal here in Florida this year (hurricanes that no longer follow meteorological models, red tide devastating ocean life, etc.), I grow increasingly worried for my children’s future. Is there anything that can be done, magically speaking, to protect them from environmental disasters in the coming decades? Or better yet, is there anything that can be done to help bring healing to the environment (or should I hang on for future ecosophia posts to find out)?

Second, the question I was inspired to ask by the previous discussion on Marian apparitions: I’ve wondered for some time about deities that have made their way into Christianity as saints, such as the West African deities that were given Christian names in systems such as Santeria, or the goddess Brigid becoming St. Bridget. To what extent do you think it’s possible to discern just how much overlap exists between different pantheons—or does it very much matter? So for instance, is there a way to tell whether the Archangel Michael Is the same being as Mercury or Lugh—or is he/them entirely indifferent to whether we get that right? Is Brigid unconcerned with whether I refer to her as a goddess or a saint, or is someone somewhere getting very offended at the conflation?

Thanks as always for fielding our questions, and for writing two very enjoyable blogs!

Best,
Ryan M.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Your children are going to come of age in a world in serious turmoil. That's baked into the cake at this point. Make sure they have good survival skills and as little sense of entitlement as you can possibly arrange, and consider relocating to someplace that's not facing quite so much in the way of environmental blowback.

2) Nobody knows. Remember that human beings are not that bright, all things considered, and we only have very sketchy information about how gods and other spiritual beings relate to one another. The rule that seems to work is to treat each being as an individual, and respect the traditions -- if you invoke Lugh, in other words, invoke Lugh, and don't go in for the bad habit of yelling out dozens of supposedly identical divine names or applying to one deity lore or rituals that are appropriate to another.

comments.title: Middle Pillar
comments.content: I've noticed definite positive personal change(s) with the Middle Pillar exercise. Are there different variations of the exercise? The Golden Dawn is the one I am using, but it seems to have several subtle differences from the heathen/pagan exercise posted on this site (if I am remembering correctly). I'm assuming there are good reasons for this, or else I am misunderstanding something. My question would be, are they still substantially the same exercise?

replies.title: Re: Middle Pillar
replies.content: They are indeed the same exercise. As with most such exercises, there are different versions, varying in detail, which have been adapted by different practitioners on the basis of their own experiences. If you're satisfied with the standard Golden Dawn version, then keep at it -- I used that for twenty years, with very good results.
